fj: Autoscaling means I never have to think about cluster size again.
fs: Autoscaling can't help a skewed stage where one task is the bottleneck, fixed-parallelism stages, or very short jobs, and it won't fix small-files or poor pruning — those need data fixes, not more workers.

followups.q: Why does Databricks optimized autoscaling scale up fast but down slowly?
followups.a: Scaling up aggressively gets idle workers onto a backlog of queued tasks quickly so jobs don't stall. Scaling down gradually avoids thrashing — yanking workers the moment a stage finishes would force re-acquisition (and re-shuffle of cached data) for the next stage. The asymmetry trades a little extra idle cost for stability and avoids repeated provisioning churn.

followups.q: Why won't autoscaling help a stage with fixed parallelism?
followups.a: If a stage has, say, only 4 partitions of work, adding workers beyond 4 leaves the extras with nothing to do — parallelism is capped by partition count, not worker count. The fix is upstream: repartition to more partitions (or let AQE coalesce/split) so the work can spread. More workers only help when there's parallelizable work waiting.

followups.q: When is fixed-size compute actually preferable to autoscaling?
followups.a: For steady, predictable workloads where the cluster runs near full utilization the whole time, autoscaling just adds scale-up/down churn for no benefit. Short jobs also suffer because scale-up lag can eat the whole runtime before new workers join. In those cases a right-sized fixed cluster is simpler and avoids the latency of reacting to load.

q: A Spark stage on Databricks has one task running long after all others finished. What's happening and how do you fix it?
a: That's data skew: one partition holds far more rows than the others (a hot join key, a null-heavy key, or skewed groupBy), so its task dominates wall-clock time while the cluster sits mostly idle. First confirm in the Spark UI by comparing per-task input/shuffle bytes. AQE skew join handling splits oversized skewed partitions automatically and is on by default, so verify it's enabled; for stubborn cases add salting (append a random bucket to the key, join, then aggregate) or isolate and broadcast the hot keys separately. Adding workers does not help because the bottleneck is a single partition, not total capacity.
fj: I'll add more executors so the slow task gets more resources.
fs: More executors don't help one oversized partition. Let AQE skew handling split it, or salt the hot key so its rows spread across tasks — the bottleneck is skew, not capacity.

code.label: Salt a hot join key
lines: from pyspark.sql import functions as F

followups.q: How does AQE's skew join handling fix this without code changes?
followups.a: AQE detects a post-shuffle partition that's far larger than the median (over a configurable factor) and splits that skewed partition into several sub-partitions, replicating the matching side so each sub-partition joins independently. This spreads the hot key's rows across multiple tasks automatically. It's on by default, so the first move is to confirm it's enabled before hand-salting.

followups.q: When does salting beat relying on AQE for skew?
followups.a: When skew is extreme or concentrated in one or two known hot keys that AQE's splitting still can't balance, or when the skewed side isn't a simple join AQE can split (e.g. a skewed groupBy aggregation). Salting gives you explicit control: spread the hot key over N buckets, aggregate per bucket, then combine. It's more code but handles cases automatic splitting misses.

followups.q: How do you confirm in the Spark UI that skew, not slow IO, is the cause?
followups.a: In the stage's task table, sort by duration and compare the straggler's input/shuffle-read bytes against the median task. Skew shows one task with vastly larger shuffle-read or input size — it's processing more data, not running slowly. If bytes are similar but one task is slow, suspect a slow node or IO instead, which calls for a different fix.

q: What is Adaptive Query Execution (AQE) and what does it actually change at runtime?
a: AQE re-optimizes a query mid-flight using the accurate statistics available at the end of each shuffle stage, rather than relying solely on the planner's cold estimates. It does three high-value things: coalesces many small post-shuffle partitions into fewer right-sized ones (fixing over-partitioning), switches a sort-merge join to a broadcast hash join when a side turns out small enough, and splits skewed partitions so one hot task doesn't dominate. Because it reacts to real data sizes, it removes much of the manual tuning (shuffle partition counts, broadcast hints) older Spark required. It's enabled by default on Databricks.
fj: AQE just means Spark plans the query before running it, like always.
fs: AQE re-plans at runtime using post-shuffle stats — coalescing partitions, flipping to broadcast joins, and splitting skew — which static planning can't do because it lacks accurate sizes upfront.

followups.q: Why can AQE only re-optimize at shuffle boundaries and not anywhere?
followups.a: AQE needs accurate statistics, and those become available only when a shuffle stage materializes its output — at that point Spark knows the real row counts and sizes. Between shuffles the data is still being computed, so there's nothing measured to re-plan against. Each completed shuffle stage is a checkpoint where AQE can adjust the plan for the next stage.

followups.q: How does AQE's partition coalescing relate to the old `spark.sql.shuffle.partitions` knob?
followups.a: That static setting (default 200) often over-partitions, creating many tiny post-shuffle partitions with high scheduling overhead. AQE coalesces them at runtime into fewer right-sized partitions based on actual data size, so you rarely need to hand-tune the count anymore. It effectively automates what used to be a fiddly per-query setting.

followups.q: When could AQE make a worse decision than the static plan?
followups.a: Its decisions rely on the stats from completed stages, so highly non-uniform data after the measured point, or a broadcast flip on a side that turns out to spike memory on certain executors, can occasionally backfire. It's a strong default, but for a known-pathological query you may still pin a broadcast hint or disable a specific AQE rule rather than trust the runtime estimate.

q: Your Databricks job is slow and the Spark UI shows heavy spill to disk. What does that mean and how do you address it?
a: Spill means a task's working set (a sort, aggregation, or join build side) exceeded executor memory, so Spark wrote intermediate data to local disk and read it back, which is far slower than memory. Diagnose via the Spark UI spill columns on the heavy stage. Fixes: increase parallelism so each partition is smaller (often AQE-coalesced partitions are too large for the operation), reduce the data entering the operation via pushdown filters and column pruning, replace a memory-hungry join with a broadcast when one side is small, or move to a memory-optimized instance type. More partitions usually beats a bigger box because the root cause is per-task data volume.
fj: Spill is fine, it just uses disk when memory runs out.
fs: Spill is a real slowdown — disk round-trips on the hot path. I shrink per-task data with more parallelism, pushdown filters, or a broadcast join before reaching for a bigger instance.

followups.q: Why does more parallelism often beat a bigger instance for spill?
followups.a: Spill happens when a single task's working set exceeds its memory share. A bigger box raises total memory but if the same data lands in the same few large partitions, each task is still too big and still spills. Increasing partition count shrinks each task's working set so it fits in memory — addressing the root cause (per-task data volume) rather than just total capacity.

followups.q: What's the difference between memory spill and disk spill in the Spark UI?
followups.a: The UI shows two columns: spill (memory) is the in-memory size of data that was evicted, and spill (disk) is its on-disk size after serialization/compression. Disk spill is the one that costs you — it means real disk writes and reads on the hot path. Large disk spill on a stage is the signal to shrink per-task data or add memory.

followups.q: Can a broadcast join eliminate spill, and what's the risk?
followups.a: Yes — if one join side is small, broadcasting it avoids shuffling and building a large hash table that spills, since the small side fits in memory on every executor and the large side streams through. The risk is broadcasting something too big, which causes executor OOM instead of spill. Broadcast only when the side reliably fits in executor memory.

q: When does Spark broadcast a join versus shuffle both sides, and how do you influence it on Databricks?
a: Spark broadcasts the smaller side when its estimated size is under spark.sql.autoBroadcastJoinThreshold (10MB default), replicating it to every executor so the large side never shuffles — a broadcast hash join, dramatically cheaper than a sort-merge join that shuffles both sides across the network. AQE can also convert to broadcast at runtime once it sees the actual built size. You influence it by raising the threshold for a known-small dimension, adding a broadcast() hint, or ensuring stats are accurate; conversely you avoid broadcasting a table that's too large to fit in executor memory, which causes OOM. The classic win is broadcasting a small dimension against a huge fact.
fj: Bigger joins are always slow, nothing I can do.
fs: If one side fits in memory, broadcast it so the large side never shuffles — a broadcast hash join avoids the network-wide shuffle of a sort-merge join. Use a broadcast() hint or accurate stats; AQE can also flip it at runtime.

code.label: Broadcast small dim vs huge fact

followups.q: What happens if you broadcast a table that's actually too large?
followups.a: Spark collects the broadcast side to the driver, then replicates it to every executor. If it doesn't fit, you get a driver OOM during collection or executor OOM holding the copy, and the job fails. That's why the auto threshold is conservative (10MB) — raise it or add a hint only for a side you're confident fits in memory.

followups.q: Why might Spark NOT broadcast a small table even when it should?
followups.a: Usually stale or missing statistics: if the optimizer's size estimate for the side is too high (no ANALYZE run, or a complex subquery whose output size it can't estimate), it stays with a sort-merge join. Running `ANALYZE TABLE ... COMPUTE STATISTICS`, adding a `broadcast()` hint, or letting AQE re-estimate at runtime fixes it.

followups.q: How does AQE change broadcast decisions versus the static threshold?
followups.a: AQE can convert a planned sort-merge join into a broadcast hash join at runtime once a shuffle stage reveals one side is actually under the threshold — even if the cold estimate was too high. So a join the planner thought needed a full shuffle gets flipped to broadcast after measurement, without a manual hint. It catches cases static estimation gets wrong.

q: What's the role of the checkpoint in Databricks Structured Streaming, and what breaks if you delete or share it?
a: The checkpoint directory is the stream's durable state: it records source offsets (what's been read), the write-ahead log, commit markers, and any operator state for stateful queries, which together give exactly-once progress tracking across restarts. Each streaming query needs its own dedicated checkpoint location. Deleting it makes the stream forget its position, so on restart it reprocesses from the configured starting offset (duplicates or gaps); pointing two queries at the same checkpoint corrupts both. You also generally can't reuse a checkpoint after changing the query in incompatible ways, since the stored state no longer matches the new plan.
fj: If a stream is stuck, I just delete the checkpoint and restart it.
fs: Deleting the checkpoint loses the read offsets and state, causing reprocessing or data loss. Each query needs its own checkpoint, and you can't share or carelessly change-then-reuse one.

followups.q: Which query changes are safe to make while reusing the same checkpoint?
followups.a: Stateless changes are generally safe — adding a projection, changing a filter, adjusting a non-stateful map. What breaks is changing the stateful plan: altering aggregation keys, watermark semantics, the number of stateful operators, or output mode, because the stored state no longer matches. When in doubt with stateful queries, start a fresh checkpoint and accept a controlled reprocess.

followups.q: Why does each streaming query need its own dedicated checkpoint directory?
followups.a: The checkpoint stores that query's source offsets, write-ahead log, and operator state as a coherent unit. Two queries writing to the same directory interleave incompatible offset/commit records and corrupt both — neither can correctly determine what it has processed. The one-checkpoint-per-query rule keeps each stream's progress and state isolated.

followups.q: If you must reprocess from scratch, what's the safe way to do it?
followups.a: Point the query at a new checkpoint location (or clear the old one) and set the source's starting offset deliberately — `startingOffsets` for Kafka, or rely on Auto Loader re-listing. Make the sink idempotent first (MERGE on key, or txnAppId/txnVersion for Delta) so the replay doesn't produce duplicates downstream. Plan the reprocess; don't just delete the checkpoint and hope.

q: Why are sinks written via foreachBatch only at-least-once, and how do you make them exactly-once on Databricks?
a: Structured Streaming guarantees exactly-once into Delta sinks because Delta commits the batch atomically with the offset. With foreachBatch you run arbitrary code per micro-batch, and if the job fails after the external write but before the offset commits, the batch reruns and writes again — so it's at-least-once. To get effective exactly-once you make the write idempotent: for Delta targets use the txnAppId and txnVersion writer options so Delta deduplicates a replayed batch, or for external systems use an idempotent upsert (MERGE on a key, or a unique constraint) so re-applying the same batch is a no-op.
fj: foreachBatch is exactly-once because Structured Streaming handles that for me.
fs: foreachBatch is at-least-once because the external write can succeed before the offset commits, so a retry re-writes. Make it idempotent — txnAppId/txnVersion for Delta or a key-based MERGE for external sinks.

followups.q: How do `txnAppId` and `txnVersion` actually deduplicate a replayed batch?
followups.a: Delta records the highest committed `txnVersion` per `txnAppId` in the transaction log. When a batch reruns with the same appId and a version it has already committed, Delta recognizes it as a duplicate and skips the write atomically. So a foreachBatch that retries after a partial failure won't double-write to the Delta sink — the log enforces idempotency.

followups.q: Why doesn't a plain Delta sink need foreachBatch tricks to be exactly-once?
followups.a: With a native Delta sink, Structured Streaming commits the micro-batch's data and its source offsets together in one atomic Delta transaction. A failure commits both or neither, so a retry re-runs from the last committed offset without duplicating. foreachBatch breaks that atomicity because your write isn't part of the offset commit — which is why it drops to at-least-once.

followups.q: How do you make an external (non-Delta) sink effectively exactly-once?
followups.a: Make the write idempotent: upsert with a MERGE on a primary key, rely on a unique constraint so duplicate inserts no-op, or stamp each row with the batchId and dedupe on it. Then a re-applied batch is a no-op. You can't get true atomic exactly-once into an arbitrary system, but idempotent upserts give the same observable result.

q: Auto Loader directory listing mode vs file notification mode — when do you choose each?
a: In directory listing mode Auto Loader lists the source path to find new files, which needs no cloud setup and is fine for low-to-moderate file volumes, but listing cost grows with the number of files in the directory. File notification mode subscribes to cloud events (SNS/SQS, Event Grid, Pub/Sub) so new files are pushed to a queue, giving near-real-time, scalable detection independent of directory size, at the cost of provisioning the notification/queue resources and the IAM to manage them. Choose listing for simplicity and modest volume; switch to notification for high file counts, deep directories, or low-latency ingestion. Auto Loader can also auto-configure the notification resources for you.
fj: Directory listing and file notification are the same speed, just different config.
fs: Listing cost scales with directory size and is fine for modest volumes. File notification uses cloud events so detection is push-based and scales independently of file count — that's the choice for high volume or low latency.

followups.q: What IAM/cloud resources does file notification mode require?
followups.a: Auto Loader sets up (or you provide) a notification service and queue — on AWS an SNS topic plus SQS queue subscribed to the bucket's events, on Azure Event Grid plus a Storage Queue, on GCP Pub/Sub. It needs permissions to create/read those and to configure bucket event notifications. Listing mode needs none of this, which is why it's simpler for low volume.

followups.q: Does file notification mode guarantee every file is processed exactly once?
followups.a: Auto Loader still tracks processed files in its checkpoint/RocksDB state, so even if the queue delivers duplicate or out-of-order events, it dedupes against what it has already ingested. The notification just signals 'a file arrived'; the checkpoint is the source of truth for processed state. So you get exactly-once file processing regardless of mode.

followups.q: Can you switch an existing stream from listing to notification mode?
followups.a: Yes — change `cloudFiles.useNotifications` (or set up the notification config) and restart against the same checkpoint; Auto Loader keeps its processed-file state and continues without reprocessing. You'd switch when a directory grows large enough that per-run listing latency or cost becomes the bottleneck, or when you need lower detection latency.

q: What is Lakeflow Declarative Pipelines, and what changed from Delta Live Tables?
a: Lakeflow Declarative Pipelines is the current name (since the 2025 Data + AI Summit) for what was Delta Live Tables: a declarative framework where you define streaming tables and materialized views and the engine handles dependency ordering, incremental refresh, retries, lineage, and data-quality Expectations. Existing DLT code runs unchanged — it's primarily a rebrand under the broader Lakeflow umbrella (Connect for ingestion, Declarative Pipelines for transformation, Jobs for orchestration), and the core engine was donated to Apache Spark as Spark Declarative Pipelines. CDC still uses APPLY CHANGES (now also expressible via the broader API). For interview purposes, treat DLT and Lakeflow Declarative Pipelines as the same product.
fj: Lakeflow Declarative Pipelines is a brand-new product that replaces DLT, so my DLT code won't run.
fs: It's a rename/repositioning of DLT under the Lakeflow umbrella — existing DLT code runs unchanged. Same declarative engine, now also open-sourced into Apache Spark.

followups.q: What are the three pieces of the Lakeflow umbrella?
followups.a: Lakeflow Connect handles managed ingestion from sources (databases, SaaS apps) into the lakehouse; Lakeflow Declarative Pipelines (the former DLT) handles declarative transformation with streaming tables and materialized views; and Lakeflow Jobs (the former Workflows) handles orchestration of multi-task DAGs. Together they cover ingest, transform, and orchestrate under one brand.

followups.q: What does it mean that the engine was donated to Apache Spark?
followups.a: Databricks open-sourced the declarative pipelines engine as Spark Declarative Pipelines, so the core programming model (define tables/views, let the engine resolve dependencies and incremental refresh) is becoming part of open Spark rather than a Databricks-only feature. Practically, it signals the model's longevity and portability, though Databricks' managed runtime adds features around it.

followups.q: Should anything in your existing DLT code change after the rename?
followups.a: No — the rename is positioning, not a breaking change. Existing `@dlt.table`, Expectations, and `APPLY CHANGES INTO` code runs unchanged on the same engine. You may see new UI labels and the broader Lakeflow framing, but for interviews and migrations, treat DLT and Lakeflow Declarative Pipelines as the same product.

q: What are Expectations in Lakeflow Declarative Pipelines / DLT, and how do the three handling modes differ?
a: Expectations are declarative data-quality constraints attached to a table in a pipeline, each a boolean SQL condition plus a policy for failing rows. EXPECT (warn) keeps bad rows but records the violation count in pipeline metrics for monitoring. EXPECT ... ON VIOLATION DROP ROW removes failing rows from the output so downstream tables stay clean while the metric still tracks how many were dropped. EXPECT ... ON VIOLATION FAIL UPDATE aborts the pipeline update entirely, for invariants that must never be breached. The value is that quality checks live in the pipeline definition with built-in metrics, instead of being hand-rolled filters with no observability.
fj: Expectations just drop bad rows silently.
fs: Expectations have three policies — warn (keep + count), drop row, or fail the update — and all emit quality metrics. Silent dropping is only the DROP ROW mode; the violation count is always tracked.

followups.q: Where do the quality metrics from Expectations actually live?
followups.a: Every Expectation evaluation — pass/fail counts per table per run — is written to the pipeline's event log, a queryable Delta table, and surfaced in the DLT/Lakeflow UI. So you can build dashboards or alerts on violation rates over time, or query the event log to find when a quality rule started failing. The observability is built in, not bolted on.

followups.q: How would you quarantine failing rows instead of dropping or failing?
followups.a: Use a plain `@dlt.expect` (warn) so rows aren't dropped, then define a second table that filters to the failing condition and writes those rows to a quarantine table for inspection. That keeps the main table clean-ish while preserving bad rows for triage — more flexible than `expect_or_drop`, which discards them outright.

followups.q: When is `expect_or_fail` the right choice despite halting the pipeline?
followups.a: For invariants that must never be violated and where downstream data would be dangerous if wrong — e.g. a primary key that must be non-null, or a financial total that must reconcile. Failing the update stops bad data from propagating and forces a human to look. Use it sparingly; for tolerable noise, warn or drop keeps the pipeline flowing.

q: How do you implement SCD Type 2 history in a Lakeflow Declarative Pipeline?
a: Use APPLY CHANGES INTO with STORED AS SCD TYPE 2: you declare the keys, a SEQUENCE BY column for ordering, and the source change stream, and the engine maintains current and historical rows automatically, closing out old versions and opening new ones with validity windows. This replaces hand-written MERGE logic that tracks effective-from/effective-to timestamps and current-row flags, which is error-prone around out-of-order events and concurrent updates. SCD TYPE 1 (overwrite, no history) is the alternative when you only need the latest state. The SEQUENCE BY column is what makes late or out-of-order CDC events apply in the correct order.
fj: I'd write a MERGE that sets is_current = false and inserts a new row each time.
fs: APPLY CHANGES INTO ... STORED AS SCD TYPE 2 maintains the validity windows and current flags for you, ordering by SEQUENCE BY so out-of-order CDC events apply correctly — far safer than a hand-rolled MERGE.

followups.q: What does the SEQUENCE BY column do, and why is it essential for CDC?
followups.a: SEQUENCE BY tells the engine the correct ordering of change events for each key (a timestamp or monotonic version). CDC events can arrive out of order, so without it a stale update could overwrite a newer one. With it, APPLY CHANGES applies changes in logical order regardless of arrival order — closing and opening SCD2 validity windows correctly even for late events.

followups.q: How does SCD Type 1 differ from Type 2 in APPLY CHANGES?
followups.a: SCD Type 1 keeps only the current state — an update overwrites the row in place with no history. SCD Type 2 preserves history by closing the old row (setting its end-of-validity) and inserting a new current row, so you can query the table as of any past time. Choose Type 2 when you need historical/point-in-time analysis, Type 1 when only the latest value matters.

followups.q: Why is the declarative APPLY CHANGES safer than a hand-written SCD2 MERGE?
followups.a: A hand-rolled SCD2 MERGE must atomically close the prior current row and insert the new one, handle out-of-order and duplicate events, and avoid opening overlapping validity windows — subtle to get right and easy to break under concurrency. APPLY CHANGES ... STORED AS SCD TYPE 2 encapsulates that logic and ordering via SEQUENCE BY, removing a whole class of bugs.

q: What is a Databricks SQL warehouse, and how do serverless, pro, and classic differ?
a: A SQL warehouse is compute optimized for BI and SQL analytics (not arbitrary Spark code), running Photon and the DBSQL query engine behind a JDBC/ODBC endpoint for dashboards and ad-hoc SQL. Serverless warehouses start in seconds on Databricks-managed compute with no idle clusters and the highest DBU rate, ideal for spiky BI traffic. Pro warehouses run in your cloud account with most features but slower start and the cost of idle/standby. Classic is the older, most limited tier. Serverless also enables fast auto-stop and multi-cluster scale-out for concurrency, so it's the default for interactive BI where startup latency and bursty concurrency matter.
fj: A SQL warehouse is just a cluster I run my notebooks on.
fs: A SQL warehouse is Photon-backed compute for BI/SQL behind a JDBC/ODBC endpoint, not general notebook compute. Serverless starts in seconds and scales out for concurrency; pro/classic run in your account with slower starts.

followups.q: How does a SQL warehouse handle many concurrent BI users?
followups.a: It uses multi-cluster scale-out: under high concurrency the warehouse spins up additional clusters (up to a configured max) and load-balances queries across them, then scales back down when load drops. This is separate from a single query going faster — it's about serving many simultaneous dashboard users without queuing. Serverless makes this scaling near-instant.

followups.q: Why can't you run arbitrary PySpark on a SQL warehouse?
followups.a: A SQL warehouse exposes the DBSQL query engine behind a JDBC/ODBC SQL endpoint — it executes SQL, not general Spark/Python jobs. There's no notebook runtime or RDD/DataFrame API to submit arbitrary code to. For PySpark, Scala, or ML work you need an all-purpose, job, or serverless compute cluster; the warehouse is purpose-built for SQL analytics.

followups.q: When is a serverless SQL warehouse not the obvious choice over pro?
followups.a: When data residency or networking constraints require compute to run inside your own cloud account/VPC — serverless runs in Databricks' account, so a pro warehouse (in your account) may be mandated for compliance or private-link reasons. You accept slower start and idle cost in exchange for that control. Otherwise serverless's instant start and scale-out usually win for BI.

q: How would you orchestrate a multi-step nightly pipeline with Databricks Workflows / Lakeflow Jobs?
a: Define a job as a DAG of tasks with explicit dependencies, where each task is a notebook, SQL query, Python script, dbt project, or a pipeline run, and Databricks runs independent tasks in parallel and dependent ones in order. Attach an ephemeral job cluster (or serverless) so compute exists only for the run, set per-task retries with backoff, and add conditional/if-else and for-each tasks for branching and fan-out. Trigger on a schedule, a file arrival, or another job's completion, and wire failure alerts to email/Slack/PagerDuty. Use task values to pass small results between tasks and shared job clusters across tasks to avoid repeated startup.
fj: I'd chain the steps by having one notebook call the next with dbutils.notebook.run.
fs: Sequential notebook.run chaining gives no parallelism, no per-task retries, and poor observability. A Workflows/Jobs DAG runs independent tasks in parallel with per-task retries, conditional branching, and alerting.

followups.q: How do you pass a small result from one task to a later one?
followups.a: Use task values: a task sets a value with `dbutils.jobs.taskValues.set(key, value)` and a downstream task reads it with `.get(taskKey, key)`. They're meant for small control data — a computed partition date, a row count, a status flag — not large datasets, which should go through tables. This lets a task branch or parameterize based on an upstream task's output.

followups.q: How would you implement conditional or fan-out logic in a job DAG?
followups.a: Use the If/Else condition task to branch on a task value or job parameter, and the For-Each task to fan a parameterized task out over a list (e.g. run the same load per region) with bounded concurrency. That replaces hand-coded loops and branches in a driver notebook with declarative, observable, retryable DAG structure.

followups.q: What can trigger a job besides a schedule?
followups.a: Jobs can trigger on a cron/quartz schedule, on file arrival in a monitored location (event-driven ingestion), on completion of another job, or via the REST API / external orchestrator. File-arrival triggers are handy for landing-zone pipelines where you want to react to data rather than poll on a fixed clock.

q: You need to attribute Databricks spend across teams and find waste. What do you actually do?
a: Enforce tagging so every job, cluster, and warehouse carries a team/cost-center tag (via cluster policies that make the tag mandatory), then query the Unity Catalog billing system tables (system.billing.usage joined to list prices) grouped by tag, SKU, and compute type. That surfaces who spends what and which SKU — all-purpose DBUs on production jobs, idle warehouses, or oversized clusters are the usual waste. Pair it with the query history / cost system tables to find expensive recurring queries. The structural fixes follow the data: move production work to job/serverless compute, add auto-stop, right-size, and set budget alerts. Tagging plus system tables is what turns cost into accountability.
fj: I'd look at the total bill in the account console.
fs: The account total has no attribution. I enforce cost tags via cluster policies, then query system.billing.usage by tag/SKU to pinpoint waste — idle warehouses, all-purpose DBUs on jobs, oversized clusters — and fix structurally.

code.label: Attribute spend by team tag
lines: GROUP BY 1,2 ORDER BY dbus DESC;

followups.q: Why enforce the cost tag via a cluster policy rather than asking teams to add it?
followups.a: Voluntary tagging always drifts — someone forgets, and that compute becomes unattributable, exactly the spend you most want to find. A cluster policy with a `fixed` or required `custom_tags` entry makes the tag mandatory at cluster creation, so every DBU is attributable by construction. Governance you can't bypass beats a convention people skip.

followups.q: Beyond billing.usage, which system table helps find expensive queries?
followups.a: The query history system table (e.g. `system.query.history`) records per-query duration, compute, and the warehouse/user, so you can rank recurring queries by cost and find a nightly dashboard refresh or a runaway ad-hoc query driving spend. Pair it with `system.billing.usage` for compute attribution and you can target both wasteful infra and wasteful workloads.

followups.q: What are the usual top sources of waste these queries surface?
followups.a: All-purpose DBUs on scheduled production jobs (should be job/serverless), warehouses left running idle with no auto-stop, oversized or never-scaled-down clusters, and a handful of expensive recurring queries. The data points you to structural fixes — move to job compute, enable auto-stop, right-size, optimize the heavy queries — rather than guessing.

q: Why do many small files hurt a Delta table even when total data size is modest, and how do you prevent it structurally?
a: Every file carries fixed overhead: a metadata entry in the transaction log, an open/seek on read, and per-file task scheduling, so tens of thousands of tiny files make queries pay overhead that dwarfs the actual data scan, and listing/planning slows too. Streaming micro-batches and frequent small writes are the usual cause. Structurally you prevent it with optimized writes (optimizeWrite/autoCompact) so Delta coalesces files as it writes, liquid clustering so OPTIMIZE keeps file sizes healthy incrementally, and on Unity Catalog managed tables predictive optimization runs compaction automatically. The target file size is roughly 128MB to 1GB; OPTIMIZE remediates an existing mess.
fj: Small files are fine as long as the total table isn't that big.
fs: It's file count, not total size — each file adds log, IO, and scheduling overhead. Prevent with optimized writes / liquid clustering / predictive optimization; OPTIMIZE fixes an existing pile-up.

followups.q: What's the target file size and why that range?
followups.a: Roughly 128MB to 1GB. Too small and per-file overhead (log entry, open/seek, task scheduling) dominates; too large and you lose parallelism and pay to rewrite big files on small updates. The 128MB–1GB band balances scan efficiency against parallelism and write amplification — OPTIMIZE and auto-compaction target the lower end of it by default.

followups.q: How does optimizeWrite prevent small files at write time versus OPTIMIZE after?
followups.a: `optimizeWrite` (and `autoCompact`) reshuffle and coalesce data during the write so each job produces fewer, right-sized files instead of one tiny file per task. That prevents the pile-up at the source. OPTIMIZE is remediation — it compacts an existing mess of small files after the fact. Prevention is cheaper than repeated cleanup.

followups.q: Why does streaming ingestion specifically create this problem?
followups.a: Each micro-batch commits its own small set of files, so a stream triggering every few seconds produces thousands of tiny files per day. Without optimized writes or scheduled OPTIMIZE, the table accumulates them faster than queries can tolerate. Streaming-to-bronze is the classic small-files generator, which is why those tables most need auto-compaction or predictive optimization.

q: What is AI/BI Genie on Databricks, at a high level?
a: Genie is Databricks' AI/BI natural-language interface: users ask business questions in plain English in a Genie Space and it generates the SQL, runs it, and returns a results table or chart. It's grounded in Unity Catalog — using table and column descriptions, curated example queries, and business-term instructions an analyst supplies — so it's a governed, context-aware text-to-SQL experience rather than raw schema-to-LLM guessing, and it respects the asker's UC permissions. It's built into Databricks SQL and shows its reasoning steps and the SQL it generated so analysts can verify. Keep the framing light: Genie is the conversational BI layer, distinct from deep agent building in Mosaic AI.
fj: Genie lets the LLM see my whole database and write any SQL it wants.
fs: Genie is grounded in a curated Genie Space (table/column descriptions, example queries, business terms) under Unity Catalog governance and respects the asker's permissions — not unconstrained schema-to-LLM.

followups.q: What does a person actually curate in a Genie Space to make it accurate?
followups.a: You scope it to a set of tables, add clear table and column descriptions, supply curated example question→SQL pairs (the strongest accuracy lever), and define business-term instructions (e.g. what 'active customer' means). That grounding turns vague natural language into the right joins and filters instead of the LLM guessing from bare column names.

followups.q: How does Genie respect Unity Catalog permissions when answering?
followups.a: Genie runs the generated SQL as the asking user, so it executes under that user's UC grants, row filters, and column masks. A user can't coax Genie into returning rows or columns they aren't entitled to — the governance layer enforces access at query time exactly as for a hand-written query. It's not a privileged service account bypassing ACLs.

followups.q: How is Genie different from building an agent in Mosaic AI?
followups.a: Genie is a packaged conversational BI experience — text-to-SQL over curated tables with a UI that shows its SQL and reasoning. Mosaic AI is the platform for building custom agents (RAG, tool-calling, multi-step) with Model Serving, Vector Search, and MLflow. Genie is point-and-use analytics; Mosaic AI is for engineering bespoke AI applications.

q: Give me a high-level overview of Mosaic AI on Databricks and what it's for.
a: Mosaic AI is Databricks' suite for building, serving, and governing AI on top of the lakehouse. The pieces that matter at a high level: Model Serving exposes models and agents as autoscaling REST endpoints; Vector Search (AI Search) indexes embeddings synced from Delta tables for retrieval; the Agent Framework plus MLflow handle building, tracing, and evaluating agents; and Foundation Model APIs give governed access to LLMs. The throughline is Unity Catalog governance over data, models, indexes, and endpoints with lineage, so AI runs inside the same security boundary as your data. Keep it light here — deep GenAI/agent engineering is its own topic; this is just the map of what Mosaic AI covers.
fj: Mosaic AI is just Databricks' name for calling the OpenAI API.
fs: Mosaic AI is a governed stack — Model Serving, Vector Search, Agent Framework, MLflow, Foundation Model APIs — unified under Unity Catalog so models, indexes, and endpoints share data governance and lineage, not a single external API call.

followups.q: What does Model Serving give you beyond hosting a model?
followups.a: It exposes models and agents as autoscaling REST endpoints (scaling to zero when idle), with versioning, A/B traffic splitting between model versions, request/response logging to a Delta table, and UC governance over who can call the endpoint. So it's productionized serving — not just a process running your model — with the observability and access control production needs.

followups.q: How do Foundation Model APIs fit alongside your own models?
followups.a: Foundation Model APIs give governed access to hosted LLMs (pay-per-token or provisioned throughput) behind the same Model Serving interface and UC governance as your custom models. That means an agent can call a foundation model and a fine-tuned model through one governed gateway, with unified logging and access control, instead of scattering external API keys across notebooks.

followups.q: What is the throughline that ties all the Mosaic AI pieces together?
followups.a: Unity Catalog governance. The data, embeddings/vector index, models, and serving endpoints are all UC-governed objects with shared access control and lineage, so AI runs inside the same security boundary as the data it uses. The differentiator versus stitching together external services is that governance and lineage are end-to-end, not bolted on per component.

q: What is the lakehouse architecture, and what problem does it solve that a data warehouse plus a separate data lake did not?
a: The lakehouse keeps data in open files (Parquet) on cheap cloud object storage but adds a metadata/transaction layer (Delta Lake) that gives it warehouse-grade features: ACID transactions, schema enforcement, fine-grained governance, and fast BI SQL — directly on the lake. The old two-tier setup ran a data lake for cheap raw/ML storage and a separate warehouse for BI, forcing constant ETL copies between them, duplicate storage, stale data, and split governance. The lakehouse collapses that into one governed copy that serves BI, streaming, and ML from the same tables, which is the core idea behind Databricks' platform.
fj: A lakehouse is just a data lake with a nicer dashboard on top.
fs: It's a data lake plus a transaction/metadata layer (Delta) giving ACID, governance, and BI-grade SQL on open files — one governed copy serving BI, streaming, and ML, instead of copying between a lake and a separate warehouse.

followups.q: What concrete problems did the two-tier lake + warehouse setup cause?
followups.a: Constant ETL copying data from the lake into the warehouse, which meant duplicate storage, the warehouse data lagging the lake (staleness), and two separate governance/permission models to keep in sync. ML teams read the lake while BI read the warehouse, so they could see different versions of the 'same' data. The lakehouse collapses that into one governed copy.

followups.q: What technical layer makes warehouse features possible directly on lake files?
followups.a: The open table format with a transaction log — Delta Lake (or Iceberg). It adds ACID commits, schema enforcement, statistics for data skipping, and time travel on top of plain Parquet on object storage. That metadata/transaction layer is exactly what a bare data lake lacked, and it's why you can run reliable concurrent writes and fast BI SQL without a separate warehouse.

followups.q: Does the lakehouse fully replace specialized warehouses in every case?
followups.a: Not always — for pure, ultra-low-latency BI with very high concurrency on small data, a dedicated warehouse can still edge it on raw query latency. The lakehouse wins when you also need ML, streaming, large-scale or open-format data and want to avoid copies and split governance. The trade is breadth and one governed copy versus a narrowly-tuned BI engine.

q: A 4-hour Auto Loader stream into a bronze Delta table failed overnight, so the next run finds ~8 hours of backlogged files in the source bucket, tries to ingest them in one micro-batch, and the executors OOM. How do you recover safely and stop this from recurring?
a: The OOM is unbounded micro-batch size, not a too-small cluster. Bound each batch with `cloudFiles.maxFilesPerTrigger` (hard limit; default 1000) and/or `cloudFiles.maxBytesPerTrigger` (soft limit) so a backlog drains over many rate-limited batches instead of one giant one. For a scheduled catch-up job use `Trigger.AvailableNow`, which discovers files asynchronously and still honors those rate limits, processing the whole backlog across multiple micro-batches then stopping. Because Auto Loader tracks processed files in its checkpoint (RocksDB), the recovery run only picks up the new 8 hours and never reprocesses, so a simple job retry is safe. Add a task retry policy with backoff so a transient failure self-heals before paging anyone.
fj: Bump the cluster to a bigger instance type so it fits all the files in memory.
fs: Treats it as a backpressure problem: caps batch size with maxFilesPerTrigger/maxBytesPerTrigger and lets AvailableNow drain the backlog incrementally, leaning on the checkpoint for exactly-once recovery rather than scaling hardware.

code.label: Bounded catch-up ingest
lines: .trigger(availableNow=True)            # drain backlog over many batches, then stop

followups.q: Why prefer Trigger.AvailableNow over a plain batch read of the directory?
followups.a: AvailableNow runs as a streaming query, so it reuses the Auto Loader checkpoint to skip already-processed files and respects maxFilesPerTrigger/maxBytesPerTrigger to chunk the backlog. A plain batch read would re-list and re-ingest everything and has no rate limiting, reproducing the OOM.

followups.q: maxBytesPerTrigger is set but a single batch still exceeded it. Bug?
followups.a: No. maxBytesPerTrigger is a soft limit. Auto Loader adds files until it crosses one of the limits, so the last file can push the batch over the byte target. maxFilesPerTrigger is the hard limit; use it when you need a firm per-batch ceiling.

followups.q: After several days the catch-up job slows down on listing. What's the lever?
followups.a: Switch from directory-listing to file-notification mode (cloudFiles.useNotifications / managed file events) so discovery is event-driven instead of re-listing a huge prefix, and set cloudFiles.maxFileAge to expire old entries from the checkpoint state.

q: A gold aggregate table powers an exec dashboard and the contract is: never more than 1 hour stale, and on-call must be paged when it falls behind. Today nobody finds out until a stakeholder complains. How do you enforce and monitor this on Databricks?
a: Split it into scheduling, SLA detection, and data-quality detection. Schedule the producing Lakeflow Job at a cadence comfortably under 1 hour and set per-task metric thresholds: a Run duration warning so a slow/stuck run fires an event before it blows the SLA, plus on-failure notifications routed to PagerDuty/Slack. For the stay-fresh guarantee, enable Lakehouse Monitoring on the gold table, which tracks freshness and completeness against historical patterns and alerts on anomalies, and set a Databricks SQL alert on `MAX(updated_at)` so staleness is detected from the data itself, not just job status. If the table is produced by a Lakeflow Declarative Pipeline, add expectations to quarantine or fail bad rows so freshness never comes at the cost of correctness.
fj: Add a cron job and eyeball the dashboard each morning.
fs: Separates 'job ran' from 'data is fresh': pairs Lakeflow duration-warning thresholds and failure alerts with Lakehouse Monitoring freshness metrics and a data-driven SQL alert on the latest timestamp, so a silently-late table still pages.

code.label: Data-driven freshness alert (Databricks SQL)
lines: -- Alert fires when the gold table is more than 60 min stale
-- DBSQL Alert: trigger when staleness_min > 60, notify on-call

followups.q: Why isn't a job-success notification enough for a freshness SLA?
followups.a: A job can succeed while emitting stale or empty data (upstream late, partial source, no new rows). Job status proves the run finished, not that the table advanced. A freshness check on MAX(updated_at) plus Lakehouse Monitoring catches the silent-staleness case job alerts miss.

followups.q: What does the Lakeflow Run-duration warning threshold actually do?
followups.a: You set an expected completion time per task; if a run exceeds it, Databricks emits an event you can wire to a notification. It catches a long-running or stuck run early, before it breaches the downstream SLA, rather than only alerting after the job ultimately fails or times out.

followups.q: How would Declarative Pipeline expectations fit here?
followups.a: Expectations are declarative data-quality constraints (expect_or_drop / expect_or_fail). They keep bad rows out of gold so meeting the freshness clock never ships corrupt numbers, and the pipeline event log exposes the drop/fail metrics you can also alert on.

q: A 90-day backfill is running on the same shared all-purpose cluster the analytics and hourly production jobs use. The backfill pins every core, production jobs queue behind it, and SLAs slip. How do you isolate it?
a: Stop running batch backfills on shared all-purpose compute. Run the backfill on its own dedicated job cluster (or serverless jobs compute) so it gets a separate, auto-terminating resource pool that can't starve interactive or production work. Back the job clusters with an instance pool to cut startup latency without sharing live capacity, and cap the backfill's blast radius with the pool's max capacity and the job's concurrency limits. Schedule it off-peak and chunk it (e.g., per-day partitions with maxFilesPerTrigger-style bounding) so it yields between slices. Production stays on its own job clusters, giving you true compute isolation plus independent autoscaling and cost attribution instead of one contended cluster.
fj: Tell people to run the backfill at night and hope it finishes before the morning load.
fs: Reaches for compute isolation (dedicated job/serverless clusters, instance pool with capped max capacity, off-peak chunked scheduling) so the backfill and production autoscale and bill independently and can't contend for the same cores.

followups.q: Job cluster vs instance pool vs serverless for the backfill?
followups.a: A job cluster is dedicated to one run and auto-terminates, giving isolation. An instance pool keeps warm instances to cut the minutes-long startup of repeated job clusters but shares a capped capacity you must size. Serverless jobs compute removes pool/capacity management entirely and scales per job. For an isolated backfill, dedicated job clusters or serverless are cleanest.

followups.q: How do you keep the backfill itself from over-scaling and eating the cloud account?
followups.a: Cap autoscaling max workers on the job cluster, or set the instance pool's max capacity (requests beyond it fail with INSTANCE_POOL_MAX_CAPACITY_FAILURE rather than ballooning), and set the job's max_concurrent_runs so overlapping triggers don't multiply the footprint.

q: Two runs of the same Workflow overlapped last night (a slow run hadn't finished when the schedule fired the next one) and both wrote the same gold Delta table, producing duplicate rows. How do you prevent the overlap AND make the write safe even if it ever overlaps again?
a: Set the Workflow's `max_concurrent_runs = 1` (already the default for new jobs) so a queued trigger waits instead of running a second copy in parallel. But concurrency control is a guard, not the fix; a manual re-run, a retry, or a partial failure can still re-execute a slice. The real fix is making the write idempotent: replace a blind append with a Delta `MERGE` keyed on the business key so re-processing the same data updates rather than duplicates. For streaming foreachBatch writes, use the `txnAppId`/`txnVersion` DataFrameWriter options so Delta detects and ignores duplicate batch writes. Idempotency means correctness no longer depends on perfect scheduling.
fj: Just turn off the schedule overlap so two runs never start at once.
fs: Treats max_concurrent_runs=1 as a guard and a keyed idempotent MERGE (or txnAppId/txnVersion) as the actual correctness fix, since retries and manual re-runs can re-execute regardless of concurrency.

code.label: Idempotent upsert on the business key
lines: ON t.order_id = s.order_id        -- business key, not row identity

followups.q: max_concurrent_runs is already 1 -- why did the runs still overlap in some setups?
followups.a: Overlap usually comes from a path that bypasses the limit: a continuous/streaming trigger, separate manual run-now invocations, or a different job pointing at the same table. The setting caps concurrent runs of that one job; it doesn't coordinate other writers, which is exactly why the write itself must be idempotent.

followups.q: When would you reach for txnAppId/txnVersion instead of a MERGE?
followups.a: In foreachBatch streaming sinks where you write the micro-batch DataFrame directly. Tagging each write with a stable txnAppId and monotonic txnVersion lets Delta recognize a replayed batch after a restart and skip it, giving idempotent appends without restructuring the write as a keyed merge.

followups.q: Two concurrent writers now hit ConcurrentAppendException. Why, and the fix?
followups.a: Delta's optimistic concurrency detects conflicting commits to the same files. Narrow each writer to disjoint data (partition predicates so they touch different files), or serialize them; raising isolation to Serializable prevents reordering but doesn't make genuinely conflicting writes coexist. The durable answer is still one writer per partition plus idempotent merges.

q: Your hourly Structured Streaming aggregate counts events per hour, but mobile clients buffer offline and events can arrive up to 2 hours after their event-time. The current job buckets by processing-time-ish logic and the hourly counts come out low. Fix it on Databricks.
a: Aggregate by event-time windows and set a watermark to the real lateness bound: `groupBy(window(eventTime, '1 hour'))` with `withWatermark('eventTime', '2 hours')`. The watermark trails the max event-time by 2 hours, so events inside that bound update their correct hour bucket; events later than the watermark are dropped, and state for closed windows is evicted so the job doesn't grow unbounded. Note the output-mode tradeoff: append mode only emits a window's final result after the watermark passes, so each hour is delayed ~2 hours but never restated; update mode emits running counts immediately and revises them as late data lands. Pick update (or foreachBatch with an idempotent MERGE into the gold table) when downstream needs early-but-correctable numbers. Don't shrink the watermark to chase latency or you'll silently drop the late events causing the undercount.
fj: Switch from processing-time to event-time windows and call it done.
fs: Tunes the watermark to the actual 2-hour lateness, names the append-delay vs update-restatement output-mode tradeoff, and lands restated counts idempotently rather than just dropping late data to keep latency low.

code.label: Event-time windowed count with watermark
lines: # update mode: early counts, revised as late data lands

followups.q: Why does append output mode delay every hour by ~2 hours here?
followups.a: In append mode a windowed aggregate's row is only final once no more late data can change it, which is when the watermark passes the window's end. With a 2-hour watermark, the 1pm bucket isn't emitted until ~3pm. It's never restated, but it's late, which is the cost of the no-restatement guarantee.

followups.q: What's the risk of setting the watermark to 30 minutes to cut latency?
followups.a: Events arriving 31 minutes to 2 hours late fall outside the threshold and get dropped from the aggregate, recreating the exact undercount you're fixing. The watermark must cover the real lateness; you trade latency for completeness, not the other way around.

followups.q: Update mode emits the same window multiple times. How do you avoid duplicates downstream?
followups.a: Sink via foreachBatch and MERGE on the window key (and any group keys) so each emission upserts that window's latest count instead of appending. That makes the restating output idempotent, so re-emissions and stream restarts converge to one correct row per window.
